skill.name: project-development
skill.description: Guides planning and implementation of LLM-assisted projects, batch pipelines, task-model fit checks, cost estimates, and structured output workflows.
skill.summary: Plan LLM projects with staged pipelines, cost awareness, and agent-assisted development practices.
skill.author: ChakshuGautam

security_audit.summary: Static findings for external commands, weak crypto, network access, and credential patterns were mostly false positives from Markdown examples and terminology. The skill is not blocked, but its Python template can write and delete local files, and the batch identifier is not validated before path construction. Publish with a warning that users should review paths and customize external data and LLM calls safely.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem Writes and Cleanup Deletes
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The executable template creates output directories, writes raw data, prompts, responses, parsed results, and rendered HTML. The clean stage deletes generated stage files. This is legitimate pipeline behavior, but users should review paths before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file write and unlink calls are directly visible in the template. The operations use relative project paths and appear intended, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Unvalidated Batch Identifier Used in Paths
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The CLI batch identifier is joined into data and output paths without normalization or allowlist validation. A user-supplied value could move reads, writes, or cleanup outside the intended data and output directories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The data flow from --batch-id to Path joins is clear. Exploitability depends on a user running the template with unsafe input, so the finding is medium risk.

medium_findings.title: Untrusted Content Inserted Into LLM Prompt
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The template inserts item title and content directly into the prompt. If inputs come from untrusted sources, prompt injection can distort model output or break downstream parsing.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The template directly formats source fields into the model prompt. The risk is contextual because this is a starter template, but it is relevant for LLM pipeline security.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as External Commands
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Static external command detections are Markdown backticks and documentation examples, not Ruby or shell execution by the skill. No evidence found of active subprocess execution in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are fenced Markdown blocks or prose examples. The executable Python template contains no subprocess, shell, or backtick execution.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs Misclassified as Network Behavior
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Hardcoded URLs appear in reference citations and external resource links. They are not fetched by the skill or by the Python template.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible as documentation links. No active network request code exists at these cited locations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Terminology Matches
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Weak cryptography detections appear to be triggered by words such as tokens, cache, scores, model names, or parsing examples. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar cryptographic use in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed context around representative flagged areas concerns token counts, cache economics, and cost estimates. I did not find cryptographic algorithm implementation evidence.

low_findings.title: Sensitive Key File Alert Is a Naming False Positive
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The sensitive file alert corresponds to fields and variables for key points in parsed LLM output, not certificate files, private keys, or credential material.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The nearby code defines key_points as output data from analysis. No certificate, private key, or secret loading behavior is present.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Against Marketplace Audit Not Found
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The reviewed files include ordinary prompt templates and parsing instructions, but no text claims system authority, asks reviewers to skip analysis, or attempts to override marketplace evaluation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious-looking instructions are examples for user-facing LLM outputs. They do not target this audit or instruct the evaluator to change conclusions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Relative Path File Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: The template uses relative DATA_DIR and OUTPUT_DIR roots for generated pipeline state. This is useful for local development, but path validation should be added before production use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit in the path helper functions and constants. It is a known risk when user-controlled path segments are accepted.

dangerous_patterns.title: Destructive Cleanup Operation
dangerous_patterns.description: The clean stage removes generated files with unlink. It is not malicious, but users should understand its effect before running it on real project directories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The deletion logic is directly visible. It targets a fixed set of generated filenames, which reduces but does not eliminate risk.

content.value_statement: LLM projects often fail when teams automate before testing fit, cost, and output quality. This skill guides practical pipeline design and agent-assisted iteration.
actual_capabilities: Evaluates whether a task is suitable for LLM or agent processing.
Designs staged batch pipelines using acquire, prepare, process, parse, and render steps.
Recommends file-based state, caching, retry, parsing, and resume patterns.
Provides a Python pipeline template for local customization and testing.
Explains cost estimation, worker counts, and model selection tradeoffs.
Summarizes case studies from LLM pipeline and agent system development.
limitations: Does not provide a production-ready application without customization.
Requires users to validate external data sources and API calls.
Cost estimates are approximate and depend on provider pricing.
The template needs path validation before use with untrusted input.

use_cases.title: Prototype a Batch Analysis Pipeline
use_cases.description: Turn a manual analysis task into a staged LLM workflow with cached intermediate files.

use_cases.title: Assess Agent Project Fit
use_cases.description: Decide whether a problem should use an LLM, traditional code, or a hybrid architecture.

use_cases.title: Estimate LLM Processing Cost
use_cases.description: Plan token budgets, retry buffers, and worker counts before running a large batch job.

prompt_templates.title: Check Task Fit
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for a new idea.
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate this project idea for LLM suitability. Identify inputs, outputs, error tolerance, model knowledge needs, and a manual prototype step.

prompt_templates.title: Design Pipeline Stages
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a staged pipeline for this batch task. Include acquire, prepare, process, parse, render, cache files, retry behavior, and test points.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Cost and Scale
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced planning before a production run.
prompt_templates.prompt: Estimate the cost and runtime for this LLM batch pipeline. Include token assumptions, worker count, rate limits, retry buffer, and failure handling.

prompt_templates.title: Review Architecture Tradeoffs
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this proposed LLM architecture. Compare single-agent, multi-agent, and deterministic alternatives. Flag over-engineering, security risks, and missing evaluation steps.

output_examples.input: A team wants to classify 20,000 support tickets with summaries and confidence ratings.
output_examples.output: A staged plan with sample validation, prompt preparation, parallel processing, structured parsing, cost estimate, and review checkpoints.

output_examples.input: An engineer asks whether a Slack text-to-SQL assistant needs many custom tools.
output_examples.output: A tradeoff review comparing direct file context, SQL execution, guardrails, and tool count reduction.

output_examples.input: A founder wants to process public articles into a searchable insight report.
output_examples.output: A pipeline outline covering acquisition, cleaning, LLM analysis, parser design, static report generation, and budget limits.

best_practices: Validate one representative example manually before building automation.
Keep pipeline stages separate, cacheable, and easy to rerun.
Treat generated prompts, responses, and parsed outputs as inspectable artifacts.
anti_patterns: Building a full agent system before proving the model can handle the task.
Combining acquisition, LLM calls, parsing, and rendering in one script.
Ignoring token costs and retry behavior until after a large run starts.

faq.question: What kind of projects does this skill support?
faq.answer: It supports LLM-heavy projects such as batch analysis, structured content generation, agent workflows, and cost planning.

faq.question: Does it write production code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides architecture guidance and a starter Python template that must be reviewed and customized.

faq.question: Can it help choose between LLMs and traditional code?
faq.answer: Yes. It focuses on task-model fit, deterministic requirements, error tolerance, and manual validation.

faq.question: Does it include security guidance?
faq.answer: It encourages staged design and inspection, but users must add path validation, secret handling, and prompt-injection controls.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: What should users do before running the template?
faq.answer: Review file paths, batch identifiers, data sources, API calls, and cleanup behavior in a safe test directory.
